Self-contained spec. Mockup plus instructions.

Overview and architecture What this file is and how the toolbox fits together

Build spec

Overview and architecture

What this file is

A self-contained build spec for the Sherwood Toolbox. It bundles the signed-off visual mockup with the instructions to make each tool work locally, plus a parked appendix for the benched web-server deployment. It opens in any browser with no server and no external requests.

The toolbox

One Flask app presents four tools behind a shared hub: Estimate Enhancer, Ice and Water Shield Calculator, Photo Report, and Documents. Each tool is an isolated blueprint package under toolbox/tools/<name>/. The app runs locally: a launcher serves it on 127.0.0.1 and opens the browser.

The registry is the keystone

toolbox/registry.py is the single source of truth: a list of tool specs (id, label, icon, url_prefix, blueprint, offline_capable, description). The hub tiles, the sidebar nav, and blueprint registration all read it. Adding a tool means dropping a package and adding one registry entry.

Reused, not rebuilt

  • Estimate Enhancer: the existing Flask routes and PDF logic, refactored into a blueprint.
  • IWS: the existing static calculator, vendored in.
  • Photo Report and Documents: new web forms over the existing headless restoration_common generators (zero PyQt5).

Run modes

Active: local standalone (this spec). Parked: web server (see the Web Server appendix), preserved so it can be revived without re-deriving it.

Hub The landing grid

Build spec

Estimate Enhancer

Enhance and clean up construction estimate PDFs: spell-check, image links, and code attachments.

Open

Ice and Water Shield Calculator

Calculate ice and water shield coverage from roof size, pitch, eave, and valley lengths.

Open

Photo Report

Build a branded photo report PDF from a job's images and claim information.

Open

Documents

Generate invoices and certificates of completion from job info, line items, and a signature.

Open

This is a layout preview only. Buttons and inputs are placeholders; no files are processed and nothing is saved.

Estimate Enhancer Mockup and build instructions

Build spec

Step 1. Upload estimate

Drop the estimate PDF here or pick a file. Maximum 50 MB.

Drop PDF to upload or

Step 2. Enhancement options

Choose what the enhancer should apply.

Step 3. Result

The enhanced PDF and a preview of highlighted pages appear here.

No file processed yet.

Layout preview only. Inputs are inert; no PDF is uploaded or processed.

Implementation: Estimate Enhancer

Port the existing Flask app into a mountable blueprint.

What it does

Takes a construction estimate PDF and returns an enhanced copy: spell-checked against a construction vocabulary, zero-quantity line items flagged, clickable links between "see image" references and the photo pages, user terms highlighted, and selected IRC code-reference PDFs appended.

Source to port

  • EstimateEnhancer/pdf-app/app.py (module-level Flask, 8 routes, 1325 lines)
  • EstimateEnhancer/pdf-app/utils/ (markup_bridge, spell_utils, spell_vocab)
  • EstimateEnhancer/pdf-app/templates/index.html, static/
  • EstimateEnhancer/pdf-app/Add Image Links Fork (subprocess helper)

Target layout

tools/estimate_enhancer/
  __init__.py     blueprint factory, url_prefix=/estimate-enhancer
  routes.py       the 8 routes, URLs via url_for / injected base
  pdf_ops.py      analyze + process logic (PyMuPDF, spellcheck) + utils
  fork/           the "Add Image Links Fork" script, path-configured
  templates/  static/

Routes (ported as blueprint routes)

MethodPathFunction
GET/index
POST/analyzeanalyze
POST/processprocess
GET/download/<filename>download_file
GET/uploads/<filename>serve_upload
GET, POST/testtest (health)

Coupling fixes (the only real work)

  1. Absolute URLs in template JS. templates/index.html hardcodes /analyze, /process, /download/, /uploads/ (lines 616, 771, 809, 815) and /static/ (lines 7, 364). Inject the blueprint base path into the page (a data-base attribute or a JS const from url_for) and build requests as ${BASE}/analyze. Swap static refs to url_for('estimate_enhancer.static', ...).
  2. Image-link payload. build_image_link_payload emits an absolute /uploads/{file}#page=N (app.py:765). Prefix it with the blueprint base.
  3. Filesystem paths. UPLOAD_FOLDER='uploads' and ATTACHMENTS_FOLDER='attachments' (app.py:57-58) are cwd-relative. Read them from toolbox/config.py (absolute paths).
  4. Fork path. process_with_fork resolves the helper relative to the file then falls back to ~/Documents/App Projects/... (app.py:1260-1262). Replace with a single ADD_IMAGE_LINKS_FORK_PATH from config pointing at tools/estimate_enhancer/fork/. It is invoked python3 <fork> <input_pdf> with PDF_HIGHLIGHT_RULES_JSON in the env.

Dependencies

PyMuPDF (fitz), pypdf, pyspellchecker. All have Linux wheels.

Ice and Water Shield Calculator Mockup and build instructions

Build spec

Project and roof inputs

Same fields as the standalone Ice Barrier Calculator.

SQ
inches
/12
LF
LF
inches

Calculation Results

Actual square footage and full-roll figures appear side by side.

Actual SF

0.0 SF
Eave Calc: pending
Valley Calc: pending
Felt Reduction: pending

Full Roll

0.0 SF
Eave Calc: pending
Valley Calc: pending
Felt Reduction: pending

Layout preview only. The Calculate button does not compute anything in this mockup.

Implementation: Ice and Water Shield Calculator

Pure static. The least work of the four.

What it does

Client-side calculator: from roof size, pitch, eave and valley lengths, soffit depth, wall and stud parameters it computes ice-and-water-shield coverage in two modes (Actual SF and Full Roll). All math runs in the browser; no backend.

Source to vendor

  • ~/.local/src/SyncedViruses/VanguardIWSCalc/src/ -> tools/iws/static/ (index.html, css/, js/calculator.js, assets/).

The src/ already carries the Sherwood logo and the title "Ice Barrier Calculator"; it contains zero vanguard strings. The rename is satisfied by not carrying the old VanguardIWSCalc directory name into the toolbox.

Target

tools/iws/
  __init__.py     blueprint, serves the calculator under /iws
  static/         the vendored src (html/js/css/assets)

The blueprint serves static/index.html at /iws and the assets beside it. To share the toolbox chrome, wrap the calculator body in base.html; to keep it verbatim, serve it as-is inside the content frame. Wrapping is preferred for a consistent sidebar.

Input fields (mirror in the chrome if wrapped)

Project name, address, roof size (SQ), inside exterior wall (in), predominant pitch (/12), eave length (LF), valley length (LF), soffit depth (in), stud size (2x4 / 2x6), exterior type (vinyl / wood / stucco / brick), calculation mode (eave+valley / eave only).

Edit point

The coverage math lives in tools/iws/static/js/calculator.js. Change the formula there; no Python involved.

Photo Report Mockup and build instructions

Build spec

Fetch from CRM (optional)

Paste a CRM job URL to auto-fill customer, claim, and location.

Company and job

Same inputs as the desktop Photo Report Generator.

Photos

Select the job photo folder, then choose which images to include.

Photo 1
Photo 2
Photo 3
Photo 4
Photo 5
Photo 6
MB

Layout preview only. This page sketches the planned web form; the working tool is still the desktop PyQt5 app.

Implementation: Photo Report

New blueprint over the existing headless generator. No PyQt5.

What it does

Builds a branded photo-report PDF from a folder of job images plus claim information. Images are resized and JPEG-compressed to fit a max file size; the PDF carries company branding, logo, header, footer, and one image per page with auto-detected EXIF dates.

Existing code to call (headless, no Qt)

  • restoration_common.PhotoReportPDF(output_path, job_info, company_config, logo_path=None) then .generate(image_files, progress_callback=None, max_size_mb=...).
  • restoration_common.load_companies(), get_company_by_id(), find_logo(), get_image_files(), generate_output_filename().
  • restoration_common.fetch_job_info_from_url(url) for CRM prefill.

job_info dict keys

customer_name, claim_number, job_location, job_id, photo_title, photo_date (optional; auto-detected from EXIF if blank).

Target

tools/photo_report/
  __init__.py     blueprint, url_prefix=/photo-report
  routes.py       GET form; POST -> PhotoReportPDF.generate -> send_file(pdf)
  forms.py        field set + validation
  templates/      form (layout seed: mockup photo-report page)

Web form -> generator mapping

  • Company dropdown -> company_config via get_company_by_id.
  • Job fields -> the job_info dict above.
  • Photos: <input type=file multiple> uploaded to a temp dir -> the image_files list. (The desktop folder picker becomes a multi-file upload.)
  • Max file size (MB) -> max_size_mb.
  • "Fetch from CRM" -> fetch_job_info_from_url, prefills the job fields. Network feature: see graceful degradation.

Output

Stream the PDF back with send_file(..., as_attachment=True); filename from generate_output_filename. No server-side retention needed.

Documents Mockup and build instructions

Build spec

Fetch from CRM (optional)

Paste a CRM job URL to auto-fill the customer and claim fields.

Company and customer

Same inputs as the desktop Document Generator.

Invoice details

Auto-numbered from the job number. Add line items below.

DescriptionQtyUnit priceTotal
0.00
0.00
Grand total0.00

Signature

Use the saved company signature or upload a custom image.

Signature preview

Layout preview only. This page sketches the planned web form; the working tool is still the desktop PyQt5 app.

Implementation: Documents

New blueprint; two PDFs per submit. No PyQt5.

What it does

Generates an Invoice PDF and a Certificate of Completion PDF from job info, line items, and an optional signature. Line items can include a base charge, preset ITEL (199.80) and NTS (150.00) lines, custom rows, or a CSV import, with a running grand total.

Existing code to call (headless, no Qt)

  • restoration_common.InvoicePDFGenerator(output_path, company_config, job_info, line_items, logo_path=None, signature_path=None, signature_name=None) then .generate().
  • restoration_common.COCPDFGenerator(...) then .generate() (same inputs).
  • load_companies, get_company_by_id, get_signature_path / save_signature_for_company, fetch_job_info_from_url.

job_info dict keys

customer_name, street, city_state_zip, job_number (required), insurance_claim, sales_rep, note, invoice_number (auto from job_number), invoice_date (default today), terms (default from company), invoice_notes, base_charge ({description, amount}).

line_items

A list of (description, amount) tuples assembled from: the base charge (if non-zero), the ITEL and NTS checkboxes, custom table rows, and any CSV import.

Target

tools/documents/
  __init__.py     blueprint, url_prefix=/documents
  routes.py       POST -> Invoice + COC generate -> send_file(zip)
  forms.py        fields, line-item table, signature handling
  templates/      form (layout seed: mockup documents page)

Signature

Saved signatures are base64 PNGs in ~/.config/restoration_toolkit/signatures.json; resolve with get_signature_path to a temp PNG and pass as signature_path. A custom upload overrides it.

Output

Generate both PDFs to a temp dir, zip them, send_file the zip. Validation: customer_name and job_number required.

Run locally Install, launch, offline behavior

Build spec

Run locally

Install (one time, needs network)

cd ~/Documents/projects/SherwoodToolbox
./run/install-standalone.sh

The script creates a venv, runs pip install . (toolbox + restoration-common; PyQt5 is not pulled in because only the headless core is imported), installs sherwood-toolbox.desktop into ~/.local/share/applications/, appends a sherwood-toolbox alias to ~/.zshrc, and records the alias in opencodeAssets/aliasDirectory.txt.

Launch (offline after install)

  • GNOME: open "Sherwood Toolbox" from the app grid.
  • Terminal: sherwood-toolbox.

The launcher runs run/standalone.py: create_app(offline=True), waitress on a free 127.0.0.1 port, then opens the default browser. Serving over http://127.0.0.1 avoids the Flatpak file:// sandbox that blocks sibling files, so multi-page navigation and uploads work normally.

Offline behavior

core/capabilities.py checks network reachability, CRM credentials (~/.config/photo_report_generator/crm.ini), and the fork script. Templates read those flags and disable or annotate features rather than erroring. "Fetch from CRM" returns a clear "offline or not configured" message; every other feature works with no network. The launcher sets OFFLINE=1 to skip network probes entirely.

Data locations

  • Companies and signatures: ~/.config/restoration_toolkit/.
  • CRM credentials: ~/.config/photo_report_generator/crm.ini.
  • Shipped default companies and logos: inside the restoration_common package.

Maintainability How the structure stays editable

Build spec

Maintainability and LLM-navigability

The structure is designed so a future edit lands in a small, findable file.

STRUCTURE.md is the map

Read it first. One row per meaningful path: its responsibility and "edit here when you want to change X." Examples: tools/iws/static/js/calculator.js for the coverage math; toolbox/registry.py to add or remove a tool; tools/documents/templates for invoice fields.

Single sources of truth

  • toolbox/registry.py for the set of tools (tiles, nav, registration).
  • core/templates/base.html + core/static/css/toolbox.css for chrome and styling, defined once; tools extend rather than duplicate.
  • toolbox/config.py for paths and flags.

Conventions

  • Predictable names so the edit target is found by path: HTTP in routes.py, logic in *_ops.py, forms in forms.py, PDF layout reused from restoration_common.
  • Around 300 lines per file; split by concern. Any unavoidably long file carries # === SECTION: name === banners that grep finds.
  • Every generated artifact (this spec file, any bundled asset) starts with a header naming its source and rebuild command, and is never hand-edited.

Why not one big file

The earlier self-contained mockup was 586 KB. That form is fine as a read-only artifact but a poor place to edit. The real app keeps normal multi-file templates; the single-file form survives only as this generated spec.

Web server deployment Benched, preserved for later

Build spec

Web server deployment (parked)

Benched, not deleted. Preserved here so it can be revived without re-deriving it.

Shape

The same modular-monolith Flask app, deployed as a single container instead of a local launcher. One image runs the app under a production WSGI server (gunicorn); a reverse proxy sits in front only for TLS and the domain.

deploy/
  Dockerfile          single image: gunicorn toolbox.wsgi:app
  docker-compose.yml  app + reverse proxy (TLS/domain only)
  proxy/              nginx or Traefik config

Key points to remember when reviving

  • Run gunicorn toolbox.wsgi:app (the same create_app(), with OFFLINE unset so network features are live).
  • One volume for ~/.config/restoration_toolkit (companies, signatures) and one for Estimate Enhancer uploads/attachments.
  • The proxy terminates TLS and serves the domain; the app itself stays HTTP on an internal port.
  • Because the app is one process serving all tools at path prefixes (/estimate-enhancer, /iws, /photo-report, /documents), no per-tool routing config is needed; the earlier multi-container proxy-hub is unnecessary.

Why parked

The local version covers the immediate need and works offline. The web deployment is recorded for when multi-user or remote access is wanted.
